coredata.dc:title: Silver nanoclusters and carbon dots based light-addressable sensors for multichannel detections of dopamine and glutathione and its applications in probing of parkinson's diseases

coredata.dc:description: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a common neurological disease caused by nerve cells degradation which leads to extremely low level of dopamine (DA) in patients. Therefore, ultrasensitive DA detection is particularly important for the assessment and treatment of Parkinson's patients. In this research, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors based on Ag44(SR)30 nanoclusters (AgNCs) with 5-mercapto-2-nitrobenzoic acid (MNBA) ligands were first developed for ultrasensitive and selective detection of DA. Then, hybrid nanomaterials by introducing graphene oxide (GO) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) into AgNCs were used to enhance sensing properties. AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors achieved high sensitivity (7.476 nA/μM) and low limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3, 53 nM) in the linear range 0.16–6 μM DA concentration. Besides DA, PD causes the concentration change of other analytes, such as glutathione (GSH). Multichannel detections of different analytes can provide more information in studying PD. Therefore, carbon dots (CDs) based PEC sensors were designed and achieved high sensing performances on GSH detection. Then, AgNCs/AgNPs/GO and CDs based PEC sensors were combined and extended into light-addressable sensors for multichannel detections of DA and GSH. Algorithms were used to solve interference problems to improve the measurement accuracy of DA and GSH in complex solution. Finally, PD biological model samples from mice were measured by light-addressable sensors. The relationships between the DA and GSH concentration and the PD stage were proved. Our designed light-addressable sensors exhibited advantages of multichannel detection, high sensitivity, fast response and so on. In the future, it can be expanded to detect more biological molecules to provide more information on studying PD.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a common neurological disease caused by nerve cells degradation which leads to extremely low level of dopamine (DA) in patients. Therefore, ultrasensitive DA detection is particularly important for the assessment and treatment of Parkinson's patients. In this research, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors based on Ag44(SR)30 nanoclusters (AgNCs) with 5-mercapto-2-nitrobenzoic acid (MNBA) ligands were first developed for ultrasensitive and selective detection of DA. Then, hybrid nanomaterials by introducing graphene oxide (GO) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) into AgNCs were used to enhance sensing properties. AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors achieved high sensitivity (7.476 nA/μM) and low limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3, 53 nM) in the linear range 0.16–6 μM DA concentration. Besides DA, PD causes the concentration change of other analytes, such as glutathione (GSH). Multichannel detections of different analytes can provide more information in studying PD. Therefore, carbon dots (CDs) based PEC sensors were designed and achieved high sensing performances on GSH detection. Then, AgNCs/AgNPs/GO and CDs based PEC sensors were combined and extended into light-addressable sensors for multichannel detections of DA and GSH. Algorithms were used to solve interference problems to improve the measurement accuracy of DA and GSH in complex solution. Finally, PD biological model samples from mice were measured by light-addressable sensors. The relationships between the DA and GSH concentration and the PD stage were proved. Our designed light-addressable sensors exhibited advantages of multichannel detection, high sensitivity, fast response and so on. In the future, it can be expanded to detect more biological molecules to provide more information on studying PD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: With the aging of the population, the number of Parkinson patients is increasing. Parkinson's diseases (PD) can cause degeneration of the nervous system, difficulties in limb movement and disturbances in autonomic function, which can seriously threaten the health of patients [1,2]. Since PD leads to an extremely low level of dopamine (DA), which can even reach as low as 0.01–1 μM in a patient's central nervous system, and because the DA concentration becomes lower as the disease worsens [3–5], ultrasensitive detection of DA concentration has great significance for the early diagnosis and treatment of patients with PD. The current methods used for DA detection mainly include fluorescent methods [3,6,7], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods [8–11], electrochemical methods [12–15], photoelectrochemistry (PEC) methods [16–21] and so on. In the fluorescent methods, different concentrations of analyte lead to changes in the position or intensity of nanomaterial fluorescence. Therefore, the analyte concentration is detected according to the changes in fluorescence. Zhao et al. used graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as effective probes and achieved sensitive and selective detection of dopamine DA. The limit of detection (LOD) for this sensor was 90 nM in a linear DA concentration range of 0.25–50 μM [6]. Although providing a low limit of detection (LOD), the fluorescent methods need expensive equipment and complicated operation, and the experimental results are easily disturbed by the undesirable external light. HPLC is a qualitative and quantitative analytical method that which is suitable for analyzing multiple coexisting analytes. Ribeiro et al. developed and validated an HPLC–MS/MS method and achieved simultaneous detection of levodopa, carbidopa, entacapone, and dopamine in human plasma with a LOD of 7.0 ng/mL [11]. Although the HPLC method has a good advantage in the analysis of mixtures, the cost of HPLC equipment and detection is very high. As a common detection method, the electrochemical method is based on the relationship between the electrical parameters of a solution and the concentration of analytes. Kim et al. developed gold nanoelectrode arrays and achieved the electrochemical detection of dopamine with a LOD of 5.83 μM [15]. During an electrochemical measurement, the applied signal (input) and the response signal (output) are both electric signals, and the output is easily affected by the input. Therefore, the electrochemical methods provide low sensitivity. As a new type of analysis method, PEC detection methods are based on the traditional electrochemical method. Therefore, PEC methods have inherited the advantages of a fast response and use of a simple device, but there are many differences between two methods. For the PEC detection methods, during measurement, the optical signals are used as the input signals, and the electric signals are used as the output signals. Because the type of input and output signal are different and isolated, signal interferences can be reduced, which is conducive to reduce the undesirable background noise [22,23]. Therefore, PEC methods can greatly improve the sensitivity of the sensing system [16,24]. By combining different nanomaterials, high-sensitivity detection of various analytes can be achieved. Moreover, by moving the light source, different sensor chips can be excited. And the PEC system can be easily to be expanded to a light-addressable measurement system for multichannel detections. Therefore nanomaterials-based PEC sensors with these advantages were widely used for the DA detection. In our previous research [15], PEC sensors based on BSA-AgIngS2 quantum dots were designed for the DA detection and indeed achieved good sensing performances towards DA (66.8 nM LOD).

However, the DA concentration in the PD patients will be extremely low with the increasing stages of PD, and other biological molecules can easily interfere with DA detection in a complex solution. The sensitivity and selectivity of BSA-AgIngS2 based PEC sensors cannot meet the demand of DA detection under complicated biological conditions such as serum or tissue fluid in the probing of PD. To solve these problems and better probe the PD via the detection of DA, two techniques were introduced in this paper.

First, in order to achieve ultrahigh sensitivity and good selectivity for the DA detection, Ag44(SR)30 nanoclusters (AgNCs) with 5-mercapto-2-nitrobenzoic acid (MNBA) ligands were used to fabricate PEC sensors in this paper. This is the first application of the AgNCs in DA detection. According to former studies, the sensing properties of different nanomaterials-based PEC sensors towards DA are mainly determined by the ligands of different nanomaterials [19,25,26]. MNBA-AgNCs with their strong absorption property and rich functional groups, can not only complete photoelectric conversion but also provide potential sensitivity to DA with better sensing properties. Meanwhile, AgNCs show several advantages in biological applications, such as good hydrophilicity, low toxicity and high stability [27–30]. Therefore, in this paper, AgNCs were selected to fabricate PEC sensors to detect DA. To further improve the sensing properties, different nanomaterials (GO and AgNPs) were introduced into AgNCs as hybrid nanomaterials to achieve higher sensitivity and better selectivity towards DA for the probing of PD.

Second, PD can be better understood by measuring multiple analytes. For example, PD leads to changes in the concentration of other analytes besides DA. According to the literature [31], the concentration of glutathione (GSH) also decreases with the increasing stages of PD. Therefore, multichannel detections of DA and GSH simultaneously can provide more information to further study PD. Based on our former work, carbon dots (CDs) with low toxicity, good stability and biocompatibility can be used to fabricate PEC sensors to achieve accurate detection of GSH [32].

Then, AgNCs and CDs based PEC sensors were combined and extended into a light-addressable sensing system, and multichannel detections of DA and GSH were achieved. By changing the light sources and measurement conditions towards different chip positions, DA and GSH could be easily measured at the same time easily. Data processing algorithms were used to reduce the signal interferences in the mixed solution to obtain the accurate concentration of DA and GSH.

Finally, good sensing performances of our designed sensors were found and their practicability was proved by the determination of PD biological models from mice. The PD can be studied from the relationship between the concentration of DA and GSH and the stage of PD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material and methods
Reagents and instruments
ce:section.ce:para: The list of all chemical materials was provided in the Supplementary Information (SI). All chemical materials were analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were dissolved with ultrapure water (18.2MΩ· cm at 25 °C). The absorption spectrums of different nanomaterials were measured by high sensitivity spectrometer Maya2000 (Ocean Optics, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of different nanomaterials were measured by Bruker Dimension Icon (Bruker, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of different nanomaterials were measured by JEM-2800 (JEOL Ltd, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis methods and characterizations of different nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis methods of nanomaterials (AgNCs, CDs, AgNPs, AgNCs/AgNPs, AgNCs/GO, AgNCs/AgNPs/GO) were showed as follows: AgNCs with MNBA ligands were synthesized according to the report in the literature [33]. CDs were synthesized according to our former work [34]. AgNPs were synthesized with [Ag(NH3)2]OH reagent, glucose and dextran according to Ref. [35]. Different hybrid nanomaterials were made by introducing different nanomaterials (AgNPs, GO) to AgNCs through electrostatic interaction. GO and AgNPs with different concentrations were mixed with equal amounts of AgNCs to obtain different hybrid nanomaterials. And the solution should be diluted to the same volume to ensure the concentration of AgNCs is the same. Prior to use, the hybrid nanocomposites should be sonicated at 25 °C for 1 min. Other synthesis details of different nanomaterials are shown in Supplementary Information (SI). Characterizations of the nanomaterials mentioned above were exhibited in Fig. 2, Fig. S2, Fig. S3 and Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constructions of AgNCs and CDs based light-addressable sensors
Fabrications of the AgNCs based PEC sensor chips
ce:section.ce:para: To make AgNCs based PEC sensor chips, the gold electrodes were sonicated in acetone, ultrapure water, and ethanol for 5 min sequentially first. Then, electrodes were dried by N2. The AgNCs based PEC sensors were developed by covalent bond. Cleaned Au electrodes were incubated in 7.5 mg/mL BDT (dissolved in ethanol) for 4 h to immobilize BDT on the surface of gold electrodes. After rinsed with ethanol and dried with N2, the Au electrodes were placed on the spin coater and covered with 100 μL AgNCs or other hybrid nanomaterials for 1 h to immobilize the nanomaterials on gold by BDT. Next, the excess nanomaterials were removed by spin coating. The electrodes were rinsed with ultrapure water and dried with N2. Then, the AgNCs based sensor chips were accomplished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrications of the CDs based PEC sensor chips
ce:section.ce:para: To make CDs based PEC sensor chips, the gold electrodes were sonicated in acetone, ultrapure water, and ethanol for 5 min sequentially first. Then, electrodes were dried by N2. Different from AgNCs based PEC sensors, CDs based PEC sensors were fabricated through electrostatic bonding. First, cleaned Au electrodes were immersed in MPS solution (20 mM and dissolved in 16 mM H2SO4) for 15 min to form self-assembled monolayer on the surface of the Au electrodes (MPS/AuE) with negatively charged. Then, Au electrodes (MPS/AuE) was immersed in PDDA solution for 15 min to form PDDA/MPS/AuE self-assembled monolayer, which changed the surface charge to positive. After being rinsed with ultrapure water and dried with N2, modiﬁed electrodes were covered with 100 μL CDs for 1 h. The excess nanomaterials were removed by spin coating. The CDs based PEC sensor chips were well-fabricated after being rinsed with ultrapure water and dried with N2. More details of fabrication processes are shown in SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constructions of PEC and light-addressable measurement system
ce:section.ce:para: The home-built PEC measurement system is shown in Fig. 1 A. The PEC measurement system mainly contains a potentiostat CHI 604D (CH Instruments, USA), lock-in amplifier SR830 (Stanford Research Systems, USA), LED light source (M365L2: 365 nm and 190 mW, Thorlabs, USA) and homemade PEC cell. The potentiostat supports a three-electrode system: Ag/AgCl in 3 M KCl as reference electrode, Pt electrode as counter electrode, and the fabricated sensor chips as working electrode which the contact area with the solution was 0.5 cm2. In this work, LED light was modulated by a lock-in amplifier to generate high frequency optical signals. The sensor chips can generate high frequency photocurrent signals when excited by the modulated light signals. Then, the lock-in amplifier can demodulate the high-frequency photocurrent signal to obtain the required photocurrent signals. Therefore, the low frequency noise signals which were main noise sources in detection can be eliminated [36,37]. In this way, the PEC measurement system could be used to achieve the photocurrent detection of pA-level. The computer was used to control the potentiostat and lock-in amplifier and record experimental data. The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves can be automatically collected by a serial port and homemade software. All photocurrent characteristics (amplitude, direction and bias of photocurrents) were extracted from the I-(t) curves. More details were given in SI and previous references [19,32].

With a designed PEC cell and motorized translation stage, the PEC measurement system can be easily extended into light-addressed sensors for multichannel detections, the structure of which is shown in Fig. 1B. Firstly, two fabricated sensor chips (AgNCs/BDT-AuE and CDs/PDDA/MPS-AuE) were connected and assembled into one PEC cell. The rubber ring and the screws were used to prevent leakage of the solution. Then, in order to achieve the light-addressing function, the reaction cell with two chips was fixed onto the motorized translation stage to realize the function of light-addressable detections. Different positions of the sensor chips (AgNCs or CDs) were illuminated by the LED light source by moving the motorized translation stage, as shown in Fig. 1C. By photocurrents generated from different PEC sensors on different positions, the concentration of DA and GSH can be determined in a mixed solution. Moreover, this measurement system can also be expanded to measure more analytes easily with simple modifications in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PD biological model from mice
ce:section.ce:para: Adult mice were used to make samples with different stages of PD. All the mice were C57BL/6 J species, male, 10–12 weeks old, 25–30 g weight and purchased from SPF (Beijing, Biotechnology Co,Ltd.). Three healthy mice were selected and divided into three groups randomly. First, the mice of control group were intraperitoneally injected with stroke-physiological saline solution at a dose of 25 mg/kg for 7 days. Then, the mice of middle stage PD group were injected intraperitoneally with MPTP (the concentration is 3 mg/mL and dissolved in stroke-physiological saline solution) at a dose of 25 mg/kg for 3 days. Next, the mice of heavy stage PD group were injected intraperitoneally with the same concentration of MPTP at a dose of 25 mg/kg for 7 days. Finally, the mice with different stages of PD were obtained.

Each mouse was anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection with 4% chloral hydrate, then kill rapidly. Then the brain tissue was washed and placed in physiological saline. In order to obtain the homogenization, the previously prepared tissue lysate was introduced into the brain tissue and homogenized by using a tissue grinder. Then, the homogenization was centrifuged with the speed of 12,000 r/min for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was kept to obtain the samples with different PD stages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussions
Characterizations of different hybrid nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of AgNCs, CDs and hybrid nanomaterials are exhibited in Fig. 2A and B, and Fig. S2. From the spectra, the absorption peak of AgNCs is found at a wavelength of 390 nm. The absorption peak of AgNPs occurs at 415 nm. The absorption spectra peaks of AgNCs/GO, AgNCs/AgNPs and AgNCs/AgNPs/GO were found at a wavelength of approximately 390 nm. Therefore, the introduction of different nanomaterials (GO and AgNPs) did not result in a shift of the absorption peak of AgNCs. The absorption peak of CDs occurred at 357 nm. Therefore a 365 nm LED light source was selected as the light source to excite different sensors to simplify the light-addressable experiment and achieve best performances.

As shown in Fig. 2C, AFM was used to characterize the surface morphology of AgNCs/AgNPs/GO nanocomposites, which confirmed the structure of the hybrid nanocomposites. The heights of AgNCs, AgNPs and GO were determined to be 3.5 nm, 23 nm and 1.6 nm, respectively, which is consistent with the AFM results of single AgNCs, AgNPs and GO in Fig. S3. The TEM images of AgNCs, AgNPs and GO are shown in Fig. 2D and Fig. S4. The average diameter of AgNCs and AgNPs was found to be 2.29 nm (with a standard deviation of 0.3 nm) and 23.7 nm (with a standard deviation of 3.3 nm), respectively, which are consistent with the heights described in AFM images and the synthesis literature [25,38]. As shown in the TEM and AFM images in Fig. S3C and Fig. S4B, GO is foliated, with the average height is about 1.6 nm and a diameter of more than 500 nm, which is consistent with the sizes described in the product description of XFNANO. The AFM images of CDs is showed in Fig. S3D, the size height of CDs is about 2–2.5 nm which is also consistent with the sizes described in the literature [32]. Based on the TEM and AFM characterization results, it can be concluded that AgNCs, AgNPs, GO, CDs and hybrid nanocomposites were well fabricated and well dispersed on the electrodes. Therefore, the PEC sensors can be developed by using the nanomaterials mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AgNCs based PEC sensors for DA detection
PEC sensing mechanism of AgNCs for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The I–V curve of AgNCs based PEC sensors in PBS is shown in Fig. 3 A. The results show that AgNCs have a photoelectric conversion capability, which can be used in developing of PEC sensors. In addition, the MNBA ligands of the AgNCs contain carboxyl groups, making them hydrophilic and biocompatible, which is critical for biosensing applications. The sensing mechanism of PEC sensors has been fully discussed in previous researches [16,39]. Similarly, the sensing mechanism diagram of AgNCs based PEC sensors is exhibited in Fig. 1D.

Under illumination, AgNCs with a discrete band gap can be excited to generate photoinduced electron hole pairs in the LUMO, which is unlike that found in other metal nanomaterials. These electrons can pass through nanomaterials to AuE (Ke) by tunneling processes. The electrons from AuE can tunnel to the HOMO of AgNCs to combine with the photoexcited holes (Kb). The difference between these two electron transfer processes (Ke and Kb) can determine the direction and amplitude of the photocurrent in the external circuit. When there are no oxidizers or reducers in PBS, the Ke and Kb rates depend on the HOMO, LUMO of the AgNCs and the Fermi level of the AuE (corresponding to the applied potential). There is no observable photocurrent when Ke and Kb are in dynamic equilibrium. This potential is termed as the formal potential. As seen from the I–V curve in Fig. 3A, the formal potential of the AgNCs based PEC sensors is −0.2 V. When the potential is higher than −0.2 V (Ke > Kb), more electrons will transfer from the LUMO to AuE, which generates a positive photocurrent. When the potential lower than −0.2 V is applied (Ke < Kb), the negative photocurrent will be generated for the same reason.

Fig. 3B and C showed the relationship between the photocurrent and DA concentration at the potential of +0.5 V and −0.5 V. When the DA concentration increases from 0 μM to 100 μM, the positive photocurrent increases linearly at a potential of +0.5 V, which means that the AgNCs based PEC sensors are sensitive to DA at positive potential. As shown in Fig. 3C, there is no obvious increase in negative photocurrent at a potential of −0.5 V with the increasing DA concentration. These experimental results demonstrate that DA can be detected by PEC oxidation of AgNCs into dopamine quinone, not by reduction.

According to the experimental results and reference [19], AgNCs showed sensing properties towards DA at positive potential, which means AgNCs exhibit the catalytic properties towards DA. AgNCs can catalyze DA directly, allowing DA to be oxidized by the photoexcited electron-hole pairs in the AgNCs. In most redox-based PEC sensing processes, nanomaterials played the photoelectric role as a transducer and the ligands around the nanomaterials played the catalytical role as sensing materials. A stronger photoelectric property can offer more electron-hole pairs during PEC reactions, and a stronger catalytic property can lead to more DA being oxidized. The sensing property will be decided by both photoelectric and catalytic properties. The catalytic properties towards DA were mainly determined by the specific MNBA ligands surrounding the AgNCs, which is similar with the BSA ligands around AgInS2 QDs [19]. In summary, the results showed that AgNCs with MNBA ligands can be used to fabricate the PEC sensors, which exhibited both photoelectric and catalytic properties towards DA.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) results of AgNCs/BDT/AuE sensors used for DA detection are shown in Fig. 3D. The oxidation peak of DA by AgNCs/BDT/AuE was about +0.5 V. Therefore +0.5 V was selected as the optimized positive potential in the following experiments to obtain better sensing properties.

As seen from Fig. 3B, the LOD (S/N = 3) of the AgNCs based PEC sensors is 3.01 μM. Compared with the BSA-AgInS2 based PEC sensors (LOD is 3.34 μM) [19], the LOD of the AgNCs towards DA showed a slight improvement. The potential of AgNCs in achieving ultrasensitive PEC detection of DA was proved. Because the DA concentration decreases with the increasing of the stages of PD and will, thus, be extremely low in the later periods, the sensing properties of AgNCs towards DA need to be further improved to meet the measurement requirements under PD biological conditions and obtain more information of PD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced the sensing properties of hybrid nanomaterials-based PEC sensors for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve higher sensitivity and better LOD on DA detection, different nanomaterials (AgNPs and GO) were mixed with AgNCs to obtain different hybrid nanomaterials (AgNCs/AgNPs, AgNCs/GO, AgNCs/AgNPs/GO) to improve the sensing performances of the AgNCs based PEC sensors. According to former studies [19], different nanomaterials provided different photoelectric and catalytic enhancement ratios of AgNCs, which leads to different sensing enhancement ratios.

First, the photoelectric properties enhancement of PEC sensors based on different nanomaterials was studied. The relationship between the GO and AgNPs concentration and the photocurrent amplitude in PBS is shown in Fig. S10. As can be seen that after the introduction of GO and AgNPs into AgNCs, the sensor photocurrent in PBS increased from 161.44 nA (AgNCs) up to 178.2 nA (AgNCs/AgNPs) and 170.29 nA (AgNCs/GO), respectively, which means that the photoelectric properties of AgNCs are enhanced. The AgNPs are a common kind of metal nanoparticles. As the AgNPs cannot generate photocurrent by themselves, the increase in photocurrent for AgNCs/AgNPs based PEC sensors occurs on account of the improvement of the charge separation efficiency of the AgNCs. Reports have shown that scattering, metal-semiconductor junctions and hot electrons injection from the LSPR effect for AgNPs may lead to the improved charge separation efficiency observed for when metal nanomaterials are introduced into semiconductor nanomaterials [40–42]. First, when the AgNPs are introduced into AgNCs, the optical path will increase because of the scattering characteristic of AgNPs. Therefore, AgNCs can absorb more photons and produce photoexcited electron-hole pairs, which leads to a higher photocurrent [40,41]. Second, the photoelectric properties of AgNCs can be also improved by electron transfers through metal-semiconductor interfaces by referring to previous studies [43]. Third, as shown in Fig. S2D, the absorption peak of AgNPs is found at 417 nm. Because the PEC system here uses a light source with a wavelength of 365 nm, the LSPR effect of AgNPs cannot be effectively excited in our PEC system. In conclusion, the scattering properties of AgNPs and AgNPs-AgNCs metal-semiconductor junction are the reasons for the observed improvement in the photoelectric properties of AgNCs/AgNPs based PEC sensors. Unlike AgNPs, GO serves as an electron acceptor and captures electrons from AgNCs at positive potentials [6]. As the speed of the electron tunneling processes from AgNCs to the GO is faster compared with the photoexcited electron-hole pairs recombination processes, GO can capture most of the photoexcited electrons in the AgNCs LUMO. Therefore, the charge separation efficiency of AgNCs is improved because of the inhibition of photoinduced electron-hole pairs recombination inside AgNCs.

From Fig. S10, the concentration of AgNPs and GO greatly affected the photoelectric enhancement ratio. With the increasing of AgNPs and GO concentration, the PEC sensor photocurrent first increased and then decreased. One reason for the decreasing in photocurrent is that excessive AgNPs/GO can absorb a fraction of the incident light, which decreases the excitation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs inside AgNCs. Another reason is that excessive AgNPs or GO will increase the impendence of the whole PEC system. These two reasons together will lead to an initial photocurrent increase followed by a decrease, resulting in the formation of a peak between the concentration and photocurrent.

As shown in Fig. S10, the optimum concentrations of AgNPs and GO were 0.92 μg/mL and 0.7 mg/mL, respectively, and the photocurrent enhancement ratios were 9.5% and 5.5% at the optimized concentrations, respectively. It can also be seen that the photoelectric property of AgNCs/AgNPs is better than that of AgNCs/GO. In the following experiments, AgNPs were used to improve photoelectric properties of AgNCs based PEC sensors.

Second, the enhancement of the sensing properties of different nanomaterials based PEC sensors was studied. The sensitivity of PEC sensors based on AgNCs/AgNPs and AgNCs/GO were measured at a potential of +0.5 V potential in DA solution (0–10 μM). As shown in Fig. 4 , both AgNCs/AgNPs and AgNCs/GO PEC sensors were more sensitive to DA than AgNCs based PEC sensors. According to the sensing mechanism of PEC sensors in the redox reactions, the sensing properties depend on both photoelectric properties and catalytic properties. Sensors with better photoelectric properties will offer more photoexcited electron-hole pairs to take part in redox reaction. Better catalytic performances will accelerate redox reactions between the nanomaterials and DA, which will lead to a higher oxidation efficiency for DA. Due to the limited catalytic performances and low charge separation efficiency of AgNCs, not all DA can be opened and oxidized, resulting in poor sensing properties. From the experimental results and theory analysis mentioned above, the photoelectric properties of AgNCs were improved by the use of AgNPs and GO, which can offer more electron-hole pairs in PEC reactions. In addition, AgNPs and GO can enhance the catalytic property of different nanomaterials. The literature indicates that sensors modified by GO and AgNPs showed greater catalytic ability for the oxidation of DA [19,44–46]. By introducing AgNPs and GO into AgNCs, the sensing properties of AgNCs towards DA can be improved, owing to the improvement in both the photoelectric and catalytic properties of AgNCs. As seen from Fig. 4A and B, the sensitivity of the designed PEC sensors based on different hybrid nanomaterials showed the same trend as found for the photoelectric enhancement results shown in Fig. S10: as the concentrations of AgNPs and GO increased, the sensitivity increased to the maximum and then decreased. The optimal concentrations were determined to be 0.92 μg/mL (AgNPs) and 0.7 mg/mL (GO), which is consistent with the optimal concentrations determined from the photoelectric performances of the hybrid nanomaterials sensors. The similar results have been reported in previous research [19,47]. The reasons for the decrease of sensitivity are similar to those given for the decrease in photocurrent in PBS described above.

With these optimized concentrations, the sensitivity of AgNCs/AgNPs and AgNCs/GO based sensors was determined to be 1.51 nA/μM (559% improvement ratio) and 6.33 nA/μM (2344% improvement ratio), respectively. Compared with the AgNCs based PEC sensors, the sensing performances were greatly improved. And sensors based on AgNCs/GO exhibited better sensing performances than that of the AgNCs/AgNPs sensors. Although AgNCs/AgNPs sensors can offer more electron-hole pairs, these photogenerated holes cannot be fully used in the oxidation of DA because of their limited catalytic ability. AgNCs/GO can catalyze more DA and oxidize DA by photogenerated holes to achieve higher sensitivity towards DA [19,48]. According to the research into CdS QDs based PEC sensors, although the sensors can generate electron-hole pairs under illumination, CdS nanomaterials without specific ligands show no catalytic properties towards DA. Therefore, CdS based PEC sensors showed no sensing properties towards DA [48]. In the previous research for BSA-AgInS2 based PEC sensors, although BSA-AgInS2/AuNRs based sensors showed better photoelectric properties than that of BSA-AgInS2/GO based sensors, due to the limited catalytic ability of AuNRs towards DA, BSA-AgInS2/GO based sensors showed better sensing properties than that of BSA-AgInS2/AuNRs based sensors [19].

Based on the results above, it can be concluded that the AgNCs/AgNPs based sensors showed the best photoelectric properties in PBS, and the AgNCs/GO based sensors showed the best catalytic properties towards DA. As described, the sensing performances of the PEC sensors depend on both photoelectric and catalytic properties. To further improve the sensing performances, GO and AgNPs were introduced into AgNCs together to fabricate a new type of PEC sensor for DA detection. The sensing properties can be enhanced by improving the catalytic properties (with GO) and photoelectric properties (with AgNPs), respectively.

Finally, the PEC sensors based on AgNCs/AgNPs/GO were fabricated, and the sensing properties and photoelectric properties of the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO sensors were evaluated. The photocurrent values of different hybrid nanomaterial based PEC sensors at +0.5 V are shown in Fig. S11. As seen, with the optimal concentrations of GO and AgNPs, the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors show the best photoelectric properties at +0.5 V, which indicates that the introduction of AgNPs and GO can significantly improve the photoelectric properties of the sensors.

Meanwhile, the relationship between the DA concentration and the photocurrent for AgNCs/AgNPs/GO sensors is shown in Fig. 4C. As expected, the PEC sensors exhibited the highest sensitivity (7.476 nA/μM) and the best LOD (S/N = 3, 53 nM) in a linear range for DA concentration of 0.16–6 μM. Table S1 shows the sensing properties of different DA sensors. Compared with other researches, the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO PEC sensors exhibited the best sensitivity and LOD. There are two reasons why the best sensing properties were achieved by the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO hybrid nanomaterial based PEC sensors in this work: first, AgNPs enhanced the charge separation efficiency in AgNCs and improved the photoelectric properties of the sensors. Second, GO improved the catalytic properties towards DA. Therefore, with these improvements of catalytic properties and photoelectric properties, the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors exhibited the best sensing properties for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and repeatability of the AgNCs based PEC sensors
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, in order to evaluate the selectivity of the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors, the photocurrent enhancements of sensors to different interferences were measured. The measured DA concentration was 10 μM, and the interferences concentration was 400 times larger than DA. As seen in Table S2, high concentrations of interferences did not cause a significant increase in photocurrent, especially uric acid (UA), which shows redox peaks that are very close to those found of DA in electrochemical detection [49]. Afterwards, PD will be studied by multichannel detections of DA and GSH. In this study, GSH, which has a close relationship with DA in PD detection, also did not lead to a significant increase of photocurrent. The photocurrent enhancements of AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors are found to be 74.76 nA and 3.36 nA in 10 μM DA and 4000 μM GSH, respectively. As can be seen from the literature [19], the photocurrent enhancements of BSA-AgInS2 PEC sensors are found to be 46 nA and 5 nA in 10 μM DA and 400 μM GSH, respectively. Therefore, AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors showed much better sensitivity to DA compared with BSA-AgInS2 PEC sensors. According to the sensing mechanism of AgNCs based PEC sensors mentioned above, the selectivity of AgNCs towards DA mainly arises from the specificity of the MNBA ligands surrounding the AgNCs. With the MNBA ligands, AgNCs showed good sensing properties and selectivity towards DA. To measure the repeatability of the sensors, four independent AgNCs/AgNPs/GO PEC sensors were fabricated. By comparing the photocurrents in 2 μM DA solution, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for the four sensors was calculated to be 1.14%. Therefore, the sensors in this research showed good repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs based PEC sensors for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve GSH detection and study the relationship between GSH and the stage of PD, CDs based PEC sensors were fabricated according to previous research [32]. The sensing properties of the sensors are described in detail here and in SI.

First, the I–V curve obtained for the CDs sensors in PBS is shown in Fig. S12. It can be seen that the CDs based PEC sensors exhibited good photoelectric properties towards GSH. Based on the I–V curve and previous researches, the sensors provided the highest sensitivity by PEC oxidation of GSH at a potential of +0.5 V [32]. In addition, DA showed the highest sensitivity at +0.5 V when detected by AgNCs/AgNPs/GO sensors. For the purposes of high sensing performances and multichannel detections, +0.5 V was chosen to measure the sensing properties of the CDs sensors for GSH detection in the following experiments.

The relationship between the photocurrent of CDs sensors and GSH concentration is shown in Fig. S13A. The sensors showed high sensitivity (21.79 nA/μM) and low LOD (S/N = 3, 34.3 nM) in a linear range of GSH concentration of 0.1–1.0 μM. As seen from Refs. [50], the GSH concentration in the nervous system tissue fluid for a PD patient is near 160 μM and this concentration will decrease with the increasing stages of PD. Because the LOD of the CDs based PEC sensors on GSH is 34.3 nM, CDs based PEC sensors are good enough to achieve GSH concentration detection in PD patients.

In addition, in order to evaluate the selectivity of the CDs sensors, the photocurrent responses of the sensors to different interferences was measured (the concentration of GSH was 1 μM and the concentration of interferences was 100 times larger than GSH). Fig. S13B shows that the interferences with high concentrations did not cause a significant increase in photocurrent, especially DA, which has a direct relationship with PD. The repeatability of the CDs based sensors was also evaluated. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 3.7% from four different sensors in 1 μM of GSH, which indicated good repeatability of the CDs sensors. By referring to previous research, the good selectivity and sensing properties of CDs for GSH detection are achieved due to carbon core of the CDs, not the ligands around the CDs [32]. By following our previous work, CDs based PEC sensors were developed and achieved GSH detection with good LOD and selectivity, which can be used to detect the GSH in biological samples in complicated situations, as described below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AgNCs/AgNPs/GO and CDs based light-addressable sensors for multichannel detections of DA and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: During the measurements mentioned above, the PEC sensors based on AgNCs/AgNPs/GO and CDs were fabricated and tested in solution with DA and GSH separately. Combined with designed PEC cells and motorized translation stage, different PEC sensors were extended into light-addressable sensing systems for multichannel detections of DA and GSH.

As DA and GSH are both reducers, +0.5 V was selected as the potential for achieving the best PEC oxidation in a light-addressable system to simplify the experiment conditions. As the absorption peaks of AgNCs and CDs are found at 390 nm and 357 nm, respectively, a 365 nm LED light source was used to excite different sensors. Then, different sensor chips in different positions were excited by moving the motorized translation stage, and the photocurrent of each sensor was obtained by the designed light-addressable measurement system (the details for the PEC cell and measurement system are shown in Fig. 1A and B). To evaluate the sensing properties of multichannel detections, PBS solutions mixed with different concentrations of DA and GSH were measured, shown in Fig. 5 . As seen in Fig. 5A, photocurrent amplitudes of the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors follows the DA concentration, which is consistent with description shown in Fig. 4C and D. Due to the good selectivity towards DA, the presence of GSH in mixed solution did not affect the photocurrent output of the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO sensors.

As described above, the CDs sensors showed good sensitivity and selectivity towards GSH in PBS with GSH, and no responses towards DA in PBS with DA. However, unlike the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO sensors, mixed solution with both DA and GSH greatly affected the output of the CDs sensors, as shown in Fig. 5B. Although the photocurrent amplitude still followed the GSH concentration, the photocurrent amplitude of the CDs sensor decreased as the DA concentration increased while the GSH concentration remained the same. The higher the DA concentration is, the greater the decrease in CDs sensor photocurrent. The results clearly showed that the increase in DA concentration significantly inhibited GSH detection for CDs based PEC sensors in a mixed solution. The reason for this might be that the charge conditions for a DA and GSH mixed solution are different from those found for PBS with GSH. CDs based sensors fabricated by electrostatic bonding processes were found to be more sensitive to charge conditions than AgNCs based sensors in mixed solution. Although CDs sensors did not show clear sensitivity towards DA, the changed charge conditions in solution lead to a decrease in output of the CDs sensors.

To obtain accurate GSH concentrations in the mixed solution, the least squares algorithms were used to process the data obtained from multichannel detections. Least squares algorithms were often used to construct fitting equations and carry out surface fitting in terms of modeling. First, the surface and model fit were carried out based on data of Fig. 5B by using least squares algorithms. After fitting, the surface function was obtained. Second, because the DA concentrations measured by the AgNCs/GO/AgNPs sensors were accurate, the concentrations of DA in the mixed solution can be obtained, and increased photocurrent measured by CDs based sensors can also be obtained. Finally, the GSH concentrations in the mixed solution can be calculated and extracted along with the DA concentrations and the photocurrent output of CDs sensors according to the surface fitting results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary applications of the designed light-addressable sensing system in the probing of PD with different stages
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of our designed light-addressable sensors in complicated biological environments, PD biological samples from mice with different stages of PD were developed. The concentrations of DA and GSH in these samples were measured by our designed light-addressable sensors. As the linear range of AgNCs sensors for DA detection is 0.16–6 μM, the DA concentration in mice samples can be measured directly by the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO part of the light-addressable sensors. According to references and the initial experimental results [50], the GSH concentration in the samples exceeds the linear range of the CDs sensors (0.1–1 μM). Therefore, the samples were diluted to obtain the photocurrent of CDs based sensors in the following experiments. According to the algorithms mentioned above, the GSH concentration can be calculated based on the DA concentration measured by the AgNCs/AgNPs/GO sensors and the photocurrent output from the CDs based sensors. Then the results should be processed to obtain the real concentration of GSH in these samples before diluting.

The measurement results obtained from the designed light-addressable sensors are shown in Fig. 6 . As expected, both the DA and GSH concentrations in the samples with different PD stages were smaller than that obtained in the normal samples used as a control group. Meanwhile, the concentrations of DA and GSH decreased as the PD stage progressed. The concentrations of DA and GSH in PD samples measured by light-addressable sensors were consistent with the ranges and trends described in the literatures [4,31,50].

The concentrations of DA and GSH in PD samples were also measured by commercially available DA and GSH detection kits as comparison. From Fig. 6, it can be seen that the changes of DA concentration measured by DA detection kits were much smaller than those measured by light-addressable sensor. There are no obvious differences between different PD stages. Because of the low level of DA in PD samples, the changes of DA concentration were not detectable by using DA detection kits. Because the concentration of GSH was much larger than that of DA in PD samples, the GSH concentration changes measured by GSH detection kits were almost the same as the results from designed light-addressable sensors. However, due to the lower accuracy of GSH detection kits, the error bar values of the results measured by detection kits were much larger than those measured by light-addressable sensors. Therefore, based on the descriptions above, compared with commercially available detection kits, our designed light-addressable sensors showed many advantages. First, the designed light-addressable sensors showed much better sensitivity and smaller error bars than detection kits, which indicates that a lower limit of quantitation (LOQ) and better stability. Small changes of DA and GSH concentration from different PD level can be well detected by the light-addressable sensors, while DA and GSH detection kits cannot. Second, compared with measurement processes of detection kits shown in SI, the designed light-addressable sensors have a fast response and simple operation. Therefore, the light-addressable sensors are more accessible for DA and GSH detection in probing PD.

As the DA concentration is directly related to PD, our designed light-addressable sensors can achieve ultra-high-precision DA detection, which can be used to study symptoms of PD. Meanwhile, the sensors can also be used for high-precision detection of GSH by using algorithm mentioned above to reduce the effect of interferences. Multichannel detections of DA and GSH can provide more information redundancy for PD.

These results proved that our designed light-addressable sensors can achieve multichannel detections and indeed are a promising tool for studying the relationship between PD and the concentration of different analytes (such as DA and GSH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: To study the PD by multichannel detections of DA and GSH, PEC sensors based on AgNCs with MNBA ligands were first developed in this work for DA detection. According to the PEC results, AgNCs exhibited both photoelectric and catalytic properties towards DA. GO and AgNPs were introduced into AgNCs as hybrid nanomaterials to improve the sensing properties. The results showed that AgNCs/AgNPs/GO based PEC sensors achieved a high sensitivity (7.476 nA/μM) and good LOD (53 nM) in a linear concentration range of 0.16–6 μM towards DA. Second, CDs based PEC sensors were fabricated and achieved high sensitivity (21.79 nA/μM) and good LOD (34.3 nM) in the linear range of 0.1–1 μM towards GSH. Third, AgNCs/AgNPs/GO and CDs sensors were combined and extended into light-addressable sensors for multichannel detections of DA and GSH. Algorithms were used to reduce data interferences of multichannel signals from our designed light-addressable sensors and obtain the accurate values of the concentrations of DA and GSH in a mixed solution. Finally, the practicality of our designed light-addressable sensors in multichannel detections were proved by the detection of DA and GSH in PD biological samples. The results showed that the developed light-addressable sensors exhibited better sensitivity and stability than the commercially available detection kits. And the relationship between the PD stage and DA and GSH concentration was also proved. In the future, besides DA and GSH, this designed light-addressable sensing system can be expanded to measure more analytes to study the relationship between different biomasses and the stage of PD. Such a system will be available to more accurately and comprehensively diagnose and evaluate PD via multichannel detections of different biomasses with the designed light-addressable sensors.
